text: For questions or concerns not addressed in the guidance document, the IATA Dangerous Goods Support team can be contacted at dangood@iata.org.

If the user of a wheelchair or mobility device is unable to provide the manufacturer's instructions, the operator may attempt to obtain the necessary information from the manufacturer's website or other available sources.
Determining if a wheelchair or mobility aid contains batteries is critical, as this information dictates transport methods and allows operators to comply with international transport regulations.

To prevent accidental activation during flight, operators must verify that the mobility device's power is disconnected and electrical circuits are isolated. The user should provide instructions on how to perform this process before departure.
Mobility aids must be placed in freewheel mode prior to being loaded onto the aircraft. Passengers should be asked to provide instructions on how to engage and disengage this mode before departure.

If a passenger is unable to provide instructions for device operation, the agent or operator should collaborate with the passenger, their representative, or the device manufacturer to obtain the necessary instructions before departure. Further details can be found in the IATA Guidance on the Transport of Mobility Aids for Airlines.

section: Lack of Advance Notification for Mobility Aids
text: If a passenger is unable to provide pre-notification and the operator cannot ensure that a battery-powered wheelchair or mobility aid can be carried safely, transportation may be denied.

Information regarding the isolation of electrical circuits can be found in the device manufacturer's instructions. If this information has not been provided in advance, the passenger should be asked for these details prior to loading the device.

Depending on the design of the wheelchair or mobility aid, the following methods are preferred for ensuring electrical circuits are isolated: 
1. If a key is fitted, switch off the device, remove the key, and give it to the passenger for safekeeping.
2. Remove the joystick module.
3. Separate power cable plugs or connectors as close to the batteries as possible.

If none of the above are possible, locking out the device is acceptable, although this is not recommended as it can increase the risk of a short-circuit leading to fire if not performed correctly.

section: Additional Resources for Mobility Aid Transport
text:
Further information on aviation accessibility and mobility aids can be found through the following resources:
- IATA Guidance on Passenger Accessibility
- IATA Guidance on the Transport of Mobility Aids for Airlines
- Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 concerning the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when travelling by air
- The Reduced Mobility Rights website
- The European Network for Accessible Tourism (ENAT)

Passengers with further concerns regarding battery-powered wheelchairs or mobility aids should contact their airline or national civil aviation authority.

The pilot-in-command must be informed of the location of: 1) mobility aids with installed batteries, 2) removed batteries, and 3) spare batteries (located either in the cargo compartment or, in the case of lithium batteries, in the passenger cabin).

Lithium ion batteries are regulated based on their Watt-hour (Wh) rating. Marking requirements on the outside case apply to Section I batteries manufactured after December 31, 2011, and Section IB and Section II batteries manufactured after January 1, 2009.

General methods to protect batteries against short-circuits include using fully enclosed casings for installed batteries and protecting exposed terminals or connectors with non-conductive caps, non-conductive tape, or other appropriate means.

Removed batteries must be fully enclosed in non-conductive inner packaging (such as a plastic bag), kept away from conductive items, and packed to prevent shifting that could loosen terminal caps. Outer packaging must not be used as the sole means of protecting battery terminals from damage or short-circuiting.

Specific methods for protecting battery terminals include securely attaching covers of sufficient strength, placing the battery in a rigid plastic package, or designing the battery with recessed terminals to prevent damage if the package is dropped.

All removed and spare batteries must be protected from damage and carried exclusively in the passenger cabin. Any exposed terminals, including non-shielded cable connectors, must be insulated to prevent short-circuiting.

To verify that electrical circuits have been isolated after preparing a mobility aid for air transport, the device should be placed into drive mode. It must also be verified that circuits for supplemental motorized systems, such as seating systems, are isolated to prevent inadvertent operation.
A battery-powered mobility aid must not be loaded into the aircraft if it has not been made safe for carriage.

The 'Battery-powered Wheelchair and Mobility Aid Label' consists of two parts: the left part remains with the wheelchair to indicate if the battery has been removed, and the right part is used when the battery is separated from the wheelchair.
Wheelchairs or mobility aids must be secured against movement in the cargo compartment using straps, tie-downs, or other appropriate restraint devices.
Wheelchairs and mobility aids, including their batteries, electrical cabling, and controls, must be protected from damage caused by the movement of baggage, mail, and cargo.
Battery-powered wheelchairs and mobility aids must not be stowed together with loose loaded (bulk) items within a unit load device (ULD) or a netted section of the cargo compartment.

Recommended practices for securing mobility aids include: using tie-down points; keeping the device upright; securing it to the base frame; storing removable/fragile parts in the cabin; avoiding unnecessary tilting; disengaging freewheel mode; isolating power; ensuring adequate loading clearance; avoiding over-tightening straps; and loading the device last when possible.

Loose fitting parts of a battery-powered wheelchair or mobility aid, such as armrests, footrests, or cushions, should be placed in a suitable pouch or container prior to loading in the cargo compartment to prevent loss or damage.
Passengers are recommended to provide their own suitable pouches or containers for removed parts. Many passengers prefer to carry these items in the cabin to prevent loss or damage.

Mobility aids are often custom-made and designed and tested for use in an upright position only. Manufacturers generally do not recommend turning these devices on their side, as doing so will inevitably result in damage.

Loading devices on their side in the cargo compartment or ULD creates securing challenges, as straps or other restraints will be placed across the side of the device, which is potentially its weakest point.

If an aircraft's cargo compartment is too small to load a wheelchair or mobility aid upright, operators should consider offering alternative routings or flights that can accommodate larger devices to avoid the risk of damage.

Operators require the battery type of a wheelchair or mobility aid to determine the necessary preparations for loading the device onto the aircraft. The five recognized battery types are: non-spillable, nickel-metal hydride, dry, spillable, and lithium-ion.

Lithium-ion batteries used in mobility aids must comply with the UN Manual of Tests and Criteria, Part III, subsection 38.3. Because mobility aids are stored in the aircraft hold with limited in-flight monitoring, this compliance is essential to ensure batteries can withstand pressure changes, temperature fluctuations, vibration, and shock without risking fire or thermal runaway.

The total weight of the wheelchair or mobility aid, including all installed batteries and accessories, is required to enable the operator to plan loading and determine if additional resources are needed for loading and securing the device within the aircraft.

section: Mobility Aid Dimensions and Aircraft Compatibility
text: The length, width, and height (including accessories) of the mobility aid are required to ensure the aircraft door and cargo compartment can accommodate the device. Providing these dimensions in advance allows operators to offer alternative routing or flights if the device is too large for the scheduled aircraft.
Operators must identify if the wheelchair or mobility aid has any removable or adjustable parts, such as a custom seat cushion, joystick, or headrest.

section: Managing Mobility Aid Fit and Stowage
text: If a mobility aid cannot fit in the aircraft cargo compartment, detaching removable or adjustable parts can reduce its height or width to facilitate safe accommodation. Smaller removable parts should be placed in a suitable container or bag and stowed in the cabin with the passenger.

When a battery is not protected by the design of the mobility aid, it may need to be removed to allow the device to be folded or prepared for transport. In such cases, the passenger should be prepared to provide instructions on how to remove the battery prior to loading.

This document is based on the 2025-2026 Edition of the ICAO Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air and the 67th Edition (2026) of the IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations (DGR). Further information on the DGR is available at https://www.iata.org/dgr.

Batteries powering wheelchairs and mobility aids are classified as dangerous goods for air transport. Inadvertent activation of these devices can cause friction or electrical loads, potentially leading to fire. Such batteries may be transported safely provided they meet the safety requirements detailed in the IATA DGR and ICAO Technical Instructions.

To maintain a safe regulatory regime, it is mandatory for all personnel involved in the preparation or transport of dangerous goods—including battery-powered wheelchairs and mobility aids—to successfully complete a function-specific training program.

Dangerous goods are unlikely to cause hazards when handled in compliance with IATA DGR. Effective coordination between the passenger and the airline ensures that all parties identify the device and battery types, understand proper loading and handling, and follow relevant emergency response procedures during flight or on the ground.

To avoid loading delays, instructions and information from the operator approval section of this guidance should be provided in advance. Additionally, the pilot-in-command must be informed of the location of mobility aids with installed batteries, removed batteries, and spare batteries to effectively manage potential emergencies.
The battery management function may be integrated directly into the battery or distributed across the control and drive system.

A disabled person or person with reduced mobility is any individual whose mobility is reduced due to physical disability (sensory or locomotor, permanent or temporary), intellectual disability or impairment, age, or any other cause, and who requires appropriate attention and service adaptation (Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006, Article 2).
Indicated Battery Capacity is the remaining usable battery capacity as shown to the user via a display gauge or indicator lights.

Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries, including lithium polymer batteries, are secondary rechargeable batteries where lithium is present in ionic form in the electrolyte. These are commonly used to power devices such as mobile phones, laptops, tablets, power tools, and e-bikes.

All battery-powered wheelchairs or mobility aids must be prepared to prevent unintentional activation during transport, and battery terminals must be protected from short circuits. Batteries should be securely attached to the device with electrical circuits isolated according to manufacturer instructions. If the device design cannot provide adequate protection against damage, the batteries must be removed following manufacturer instructions.

Battery-powered wheelchairs or mobility aids are classified into three main categories based on battery type: 1) Non-spillable wet batteries, nickel-metal hydride batteries, or dry batteries (DGR 2.3.2.2); 2) Spillable batteries (DGR 2.3.2.3); and 3) Lithium batteries (DGR 2.3.2.4). Each category has unique requirements that must be strictly followed to ensure safe transport.

section: Special Provision A67: Non-Spillable Wet Batteries
text: Under Special Provision A67, wet cell batteries are considered non-spillable if they can withstand vibration and pressure differential tests without leaking battery fluid. The vibration test requires the battery to be rigidly clamped to a platform with a simple harmonic motion (amplitude of 0.8 mm, maximum total excursion of 1.6 mm) applied. The frequency is varied at 1 Hz/min between 10 Hz and 55 Hz. The battery must be tested in three mutually perpendicular positions (including inverted fill openings and vents) for at least six hours in each position.

In addition to the battery installed in the wheelchair (which may be removed and carried in the cabin), a passenger may carry a maximum of one spare wet, non-spillable battery that meets Special Provision A67.

Nickel-metal hydride batteries must comply with Special Provision A199. These batteries or battery-powered devices are not subject to DGR regulations provided they are prepared to prevent short circuits. A passenger may carry a maximum of two spare nickel-metal hydride batteries meeting Special Provision A199.

Dry batteries, including alkali-manganese, zinc-carbon, and nickel-cadmium batteries, must comply with Special Provision A123. A passenger may carry a maximum of two spare dry batteries meeting Special Provision A123.

section: Transport of Removed and Spare Batteries
text: All removed and spare batteries must be carried in strong, rigid packaging and must be transported in the cargo compartment. If a wheelchair or mobility aid does not provide adequate protection for the battery, the battery must be removed.
Removed batteries must be transported in the cargo compartment and stored in strong, rigid packaging.
Lithium batteries used to power wheelchairs and mobility aids must comply with the requirements of the UN Manual of Tests and Criteria, Part III, subsection 38.3.

Lithium batteries may remain installed in a wheelchair or mobility aid if they are securely attached and protected from damage by design. Otherwise, they must be removed according to the manufacturer's instructions.

If installed batteries exceed 300 Wh, operators must implement additional safety mitigation measures. Common measures include using fire-resistant containers or covers for the mobility aid, or requiring the battery to be transported at a reduced charge of 25% or less.

Some operators require lithium batteries to be removed from mobility aids and carried in the cabin. In these instances, removed batteries and spare batteries must not exceed a cumulative total of 300 Wh per mobility aid and must be protected from damage.

IATA and aviation stakeholders have developed the 'Guidance on the Transport of Mobility Aids' to assist operators in facilitating customer transport. This includes training programs that address attitudinal, physical, and environmental factors to ensure respectful service, as well as guidance on dangerous goods acceptance, loading, and notification processes.
Prior to acceptance, operators must verify that battery terminals are protected from short circuits; this protection may be achieved without necessarily removing the battery.

Operators must confirm that relevant special provisions are complied with for specific battery types: A67 for non-spillable wet batteries, A199 for nickel-metal hydride batteries, and A123 for dry batteries.

All removed and spare batteries must be placed in strong, rigid packaging and transported only in the cargo compartment. Batteries must be removed if the mobility aid cannot be loaded, stowed, secured, or unloaded in an upright position.

Lithium-ion batteries must meet the requirements of the UN Manual of Tests and Criteria, Part III, subsection 38.3. If the batteries are removed from the mobility aid, the cumulative total must not exceed 300 Wh.

Non-spillable wet batteries have an absorbed electrolyte (e.g., absorbed glass mat (AGM), gel battery, gel cell, and sealed lead-acid (SLS) batteries) and do not leak electrolyte or liquid even if the battery case is ruptured or cracked. These batteries must be capable of passing specific vibration and pressure differential tests.
Examples of dry batteries include alkali-manganese, zinc-carbon, nickel-cadmium, and nickel-metal hydride batteries.
Operator: A person, organisation, or enterprise engaged in or offering to engage in an aircraft operation.

Spillable wet batteries consist of a series of metal plates immersed in a corrosive liquid electrolyte, such as dilute sulphuric acid. These batteries will leak the corrosive electrolyte if not maintained in an upright condition throughout transport, including loading and unloading.
Tour operator: An organiser or retailer of package tours and tourism services for sale either directly to travellers or through intermediaries.

Airlines use specific codes to communicate the type of wheelchair being transported: 
- WCBD: Wheelchair powered by a non-spillable battery, nickel-metal hydride battery, or a dry battery.
- WCBW: Wheelchair powered by a wet cell (spillable) battery.
- WCLB: Wheelchair with an installed lithium battery.
- WCLX: Wheelchair with the lithium battery removed for transportation.

Wheelchair system: The electrical and electronic traction control system for a wheelchair, including the battery, battery manager, motor speed controller, user interface, and all associated wiring and safety devices.

It is strongly recommended that wheelchair or mobility aid users, travel agents, tour operators, or third-party booking websites seek approval for the carriage of the mobility aid in advance. This ensures that safety assessments can be conducted and necessary handling arrangements can be put in place.

Operators must maintain a documented approval process published in accessible formats (such as electronic, web-based, large print, and audio) available in a timely manner and at no additional cost to the passenger. Details should be clearly described on the operator's website and available to call-centre staff. Web-based materials and internet booking must comply with international web accessibility standards (W3C).
Operators often establish specialist teams to coordinate the carriage of passengers and their mobility devices.

When transporting large batteries, operators must comply with DGR 9.1.9 (Requirement for Safety Risk Assessment) and DGR 1.4.2.2 (Recommendations regarding approval of dangerous goods carried by passengers and crew). Further guidance is provided in the IATA document 'Lithium Battery Risk Assessment Guidance for Operators'.
